text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum overlying the cervix using monopolar shears, revealing the pubic cervical fascia as the peritoneum is dissected away. Adhesiolysis is performed anteriorly to the cervix using sharp dissection and electrosurgical energy, aided by a medium malleable retractor in the vagina. Posteriorly, the peritoneum is tented and dissected off the cervix, again exposing the pubic cervical fascia. The completed anterior and posterior dissections are visible before mesh placement. Extracorporeally, the polypropylene Y mesh is prepared, with an arm sutured in an accordion fashion for intra-abdominal management. The mesh is placed over the cervical stump, and its apex is secured to the cervix with permanent interrupted sutures.

The surgeon secures the posterior aspect of the cervix to the mesh using permanent sutures. The posterior vaginal component is sutured to the mesh with vicryl suture. The anterior arm of the mesh is released from its sutures and secured over the anterior aspect of the cervix.

The surgeon secures the mesh under traction, identifying the sacral promontory and incising the overlying peritoneum. The incision is extended medially to the posterior vaginal incision to prevent ureteric injury. The thin anterior longitudinal ligament and middle sacral vessels are dissected, with the vessels desiccated using fenestrated bipolar forceps to prevent hemorrhage. The mesh is attached to the anterior longitudinal ligament at the sacrum's hollow using permanent interrupted sutures. The base of the mesh is shortened for anatomical fit, and the final mesh placement from the cervix to the sacral promontory is shown. The mesh is re-peritonealized with a two-zero V-lock suture to prevent bowel entrapment.
